title: A Wafer-Scale Heterogeneous III-V-on-Silicon Nitride Quantum Photonic Platform
authors: Lillian Thiel, Boqiang Shen, Jasper R. Venneberg, Melissa A. Guidry, Nic Arnaud, Adam Slater, Lucas Wang, Xuefeng Li, Josh Castro, Yiming Pang, Max Meunier, Sahil D. Patel, Yang Shen, Theodore Morin, Igor Kudelin, Bowen Song, Kaustubh Asawa, John E. Bowers, Kerry Vahala, Nergis Mavalvala, Xinghui Yin, Steven Bowers, Minh A. Tran, Tin Komljenovic, Galan Moody
abstract: Heterogeneous integration of gain and strongly nonlinear materials with ultra-low-loss silicon nitride (SiN) photonics offers a route to scalable quantum circuits, but concurrent wafer-scale manufacturability, low interlayer loss, and high performance have been challenging to realize. Here we demonstrate a wafer-scale III-V-on-SiN quantum photonic platform that directly integrates III-V layers to foundry-fabricated SiN circuits. The SiN layer provides 200-300 nm thick waveguides with $<1$ dB/m loss and a mature passive photonics ecosystem, while III-V materials provide large $χ^{\left(2\right)}$ and $χ^{\left(3\right)}$ nonlinearities for parametric gain, frequency conversion and quantum light generation. Adiabatic interlayer couplers yield $<25$ mdB loss to InGaP waveguides and resonators with intrinsic quality factors exceeding $10^6$, enabling $15\times$ brighter entanglement sources and efficient nonlinear conversion on SiN. Integrated components--including low-loss beam splitters, waveguide crossers, and tunable interferometers--are complemented by III-V lasers and InP photodetectors with amplifiers achieving up to $99^{+1}_{-12}\%$ quantum efficiency and $3$ GHz bandwidth. This architecture unites ultra-efficient sources, nonlinear elements and detectors on a wafer-scale, low-loss platform, establishing a path toward large-scale, low-noise quantum photonic systems.
summary: The researchers have developed a new way to manufacture quantum photonic chips by bonding III-V materials directly onto silicon nitride wafers. This platform allows for the integration of lasers, nonlinear light sources, and high-efficiency detectors on a single, large-scale chip. This advancement is a major step toward building complex, large-scale quantum computers and communication networks.
main_problem: The difficulty of creating scalable, wafer-scale quantum photonic circuits that simultaneously provide ultra-low-loss passive routing, high optical gain, and strong nonlinearities without the losses or scalability issues of previous integration methods.
main_result: The demonstration of a wafer-scale III-V-on-SiN platform that integrates high-efficiency nonlinear elements, lasers, and detectors, achieving 15x brighter entanglement sources and near-unity quantum efficiency in photodetectors.
method: Wafer-scale manufacturing using direct bonding of III-V epitaxial stacks onto foundry-fabricated SiN circuits, utilizing adiabatic inverse tapers for interlayer coupling and self-injection locking for laser noise reduction.
model_or_system: A heterogeneous photonic platform consisting of ultra-low-loss Silicon Nitride (SiN) waveguides integrated with III-V materials (InGaP and InP) for gain, nonlinearity, and detection.
key_observables: SiN waveguide loss, InGaP waveguide loss, interlayer coupling loss, SHG efficiency, entangled-photon brightness, two-photon entanglement fidelity, laser linewidth, and photodetector quantum efficiency.
important_parameters: SiN loss < 1 dB/m, InGaP SHG efficiency > 35,000 %/W, entanglement fidelity of 99.9%, photodetector quantum efficiency > 99%, and laser linewidth in the hertz-level regime.
figures_summary: Figure 4b shows the integrated homodyne detector setup and a model for squeezing degradation; Figure 4c displays photodetector bandwidth and noise; Figure 5a illustrates the vision for a fully integrated system.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of scalable quantum photonics, describes the fabrication and architecture of the III-V-on-SiN platform, details the characterization of nonlinear sources and active components, analyzes noise and efficiency in the detection system, and concludes with a roadmap for large-scale integration.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it provides a scalable hardware architecture for generating and detecting complex quantum states like squeezed light and entangled photons with extremely high fidelity and low loss.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Heterogeneous III-V-on-SiN platform. (a) Illustration of our scalable nonlinear quantum photonics platform, which includes quantum entanglement sources, nonlinear frequency conversion and generation, tunable interferometric circuits, high-efficiency integrated photodetectors, and tunable low-noise semiconductor lasers. (b) Measured waveguide propagation losses for the III-V InGaP layer and the two SiN layers, and the measured III-V-to-SiN adiabatic layer transition coupling losses near 1560 nm and 780 nm (c) Wafer fabrication flow from internal modeling, design, and layout to SiN foundry fabrication, back-end-of-line III-V integration, and wafer-scale testing. The right panel...

Figure 2 illustrates the InGaP-on-SiN chiplets and their performance for nonlinear frequency generation and entangled-photon pair sources. The fabrication process is detailed in the supplementary information. After SiO2- clad 200-mm SiN wafers are received from the foundry, they are cored and planarized, leaving a ∼50 nm SiO2 spacer between the 200-nm thick SiN and 105-nm thick InGaP layers. InGaP is direct bonded through an es- tablished process [16, 20], followed by PECVD cladding and metallization for thermal tuning. The top panel of Fig. 2a shows a chiplet with hundreds of add-drop res- onator entangled-pair sources, and the bottom panel is a zoom-in of spiral waveguides for loss...

FIG. 2. InGaP nonlinear frequency generation and entanglement sources on SiN. (a) Images of an InGaP-on- SiN singulated chip under test, including (bottom) add-drop microresonators and up to 10-cm-long spiral waveguides. (b) Representative microresonator transmission spectra near 1560 nm for resonators designed for escape efficiencies of ηesc = 0.5, 0.9, and 0.95 (left-to-right). (c) InGaP propagation loss extracted from microresonator transmission data near 1560 nm versus ring width, demonstrating loss as low as 0.7 dB/cm. (d) Measured III-V-to-SiN adiabatic transition losses for 1550 nm TE modes, demonstrating < 25 mdB coupling loss at 1560 nm. (e) Second harmonic generation (SHG)...

FIG. 3. Low-Noise and tunable integrated lasers and interferometers. (a) Fabricated SiN wafer with integrated III-V semiconductor lasers and amplifiers, with a multi-channel tunable semiconductor amplifier chip shown in the bottom panel. (b) Both 1560 nm and 780 nm tunable lasers are demonstrated with kilohertz (hertz) level linewidths without (with) injection locking, side-mode suppression ratios as high as 50 dB, and off-chip output power exceeding 10 mW are demonstrated. (c) Integrated Mach Zehnder interferometer with < 6 mdB insertion loss, > 40 dB extinction, and < 0.4 mrad interferometric phase noise (without locking) integrated across 100 kHz to 10 MHz.

FIG. 4. High quantum efficiency and low-noise photodetectors. (a) Image of completed InP-on-SiN photodetector wafer. (b) Packaged photodetector chip with low-noise transimpedance amplifier. (c) Electronic noise clearance at 1 mW on-chip optical pump power at 1550 nm and 3-dB bandwidth of the packaged detector and amplifier. (d-f) Dark current, photocurrent, and calibrated quantum efficiency of the different photodetector configurations. Quantum efficiency exceeding 99% is measured for the circular multi-bounce designs.
